:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,859 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 11% of the parole-eligible prison population.



How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Inmates already serving one sentence, whether in confinement or on parole, who reoffend and are sentenced for a new crime, are not eligible for parole until they have served one-third of the new sentence (and will not receive credit for the time they served under their previous sentence). If an inmate is sentenced for multiple subsequent offenses, parole eligibility is calculated in the same manner as described above for an original sentence involving multiple offenses.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 54% (1,005 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Overview ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, there were 1,222 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 66% of the parole-eligible prison population.
